# Dependency Injection

<!-- AUTO-INDEX:START (generated by `python -m engram topics` — do not edit by hand) -->
## Atom index (40 atoms, auto-generated from atoms.jsonl)

_Every atom under this topic with its source proof link. Regenerate with `python -m engram topics`._

### claim (5)
- **Though the idea is simple, it unlocks some very powerful side effects.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[CORE]`
- **Message text goes to a standard chat service built for speed (near real-time), while the attachment goes to the separate attachment service.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **The complete architecture is configurable from one spot, making it super easy to change — switching to Synergy is two lines, and adding preview support for a new file type just slots in.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[CORE]`
- **Injection timing varies: some dependencies are resolved and injected at startup (most common), some when a request comes in, but the process is the same — code lists the dependencies it needs and you fulfill them.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **The biggest reason to use interfaces and injection even with a single implementation is testing.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[CORE]`

### concept (3)
- **The request handler isn't aware of which storage is passed in or where the file is going — it just knows it can call upload.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[CORE]`
- **Beyond final storage, an upload passes through stages: virus scan, image scaling, preview generation, and encryption.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **Because components talk through injected interfaces, you control what's used at each connection point — including injecting fake or mock implementations to slice and isolate sections of the architecture during testing.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[CORE]`

### definition (4)
- **Dependency injection is simply when code that uses another piece of code, instead of using it directly, has that code passed in; passing it in is the 'injection'.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[CORE]`
- **The default storage location is S3, Amazon's simple storage service for putting up and pulling down files.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **The virus scanner checks files for signatures of obvious viruses.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **Preview generation makes the thumbnail shown under an attachment so the user can see what it is without fully downloading it.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`

### method (13)
- **On upload, the attachment is stored on disk temporarily, processed in several ways, then uploaded to its final destination.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **The DI solution: create a storage interface with an upload method that does what the request handler wants.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[CORE]`
- **Create three implementations of the storage interface, passing each one's configuration into its constructor — no optional variables, required values, and an error if you forget one.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[CORE]`
- **Once the user is authenticated and their company is known, create the right storage and inject only that storage into the request handler.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[CORE]`
- **Since the construction code is too complex to sit in the handler, move it out to a factory whose input is the company configuration and whose output is a storage conforming to the interface.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **If the file is an image it's scaled down to a max width of 2500 pixels, which is what's shown when a user clicks an image because it uses less bandwidth and loads faster.** (max width 2500px) — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **Files stored on S3 are pre-encrypted before upload, so a breach at Amazon only exposes encrypted files.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **Create a shared interface for the two scanners and, on initialization, pick one and inject it: Synergy in development mode, the old ThreatProtect in production.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[CORE]`
- **Image scaling (the sharp library) is wrapped in an image-scaler interface that also reports whether an attachment supports scaling, so the upload only scales when supported.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **Preview generation, the most complex stage, uses a preview-generator interface taking the input file and returning a preview image, with implementations for documents, videos, and images.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **The image preview generator reuses the same image scaler by injecting it in, rather than reimplementing scaling.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **Since only one preview generator is needed per file type, inject a factory that takes the upload's mime type and returns the right generator, so the upload just asks the factory and uses it.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **Encryption has one implementation (AES) but a per-user key from a key service, so inject the key service into the AES encryption and the AES encryption into the storage factory.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`

### example (8)
- **The running example is a business chat app where users message co-workers and send pictures and files; attachments go to an attachment service that stores, retrieves, and processes them.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **Most picky clients provide an SFTP server, but one client specifically wanted WebDAV.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **They currently use the ThreatProtect scanner but plan to switch to Synergy Security (better detection KPIs); the deal isn't finalized, so Synergy may only be tested in development, not production.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **For an AWS-configured company the storage factory injects AES encryption into the new AWS storage, and the upload calls upload without knowing about the whole chain of connected functionality.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **To test the AWS storage class, inject a fake local S3 that pretends to be the cloud, call upload, and verify a file got uploaded.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **Since encryption would block checking the file content, inject a mock encryption that disables encryption, so the test can fully verify uploading in isolation.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **To test encryption itself, mock out the key store to return a controlled key instead of going to the real key service.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **To integration-test AWS and encryption together, inject a fake keystore into the real encryption and the real encryption into the AWS storage.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`

### analogy (1)
- **Injection basically lets you pick and choose from your compatible puzzle pieces and slot them in when you need them.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[CORE]`

### caveat (2)
- **A few client firms don't want their data permanently stored, so the service must handle multiple storage locations chosen by which company a user belongs to.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
- **With no access to the key server on a local dev box, you can just inject a fixed key when running locally.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`

### opinion (1)
- **'Dependency injection' is a term the creator doesn't love because it sounds a lot fancier than it actually is.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`

### warning (3)
- **The naive approach — extend the upload code with if-statements and have the caller pass in where to upload — intermingles SFTP, AWS, and WebDAV code in one class with many paths, making it ugly and hard to understand.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[CORE]`
- **Because each destination needs different info (AWS keys vs SFTP address/private key vs WebDAV URL and auth), the naive upload forces a bunch of optional variables and comments, making it easy for the caller to make a mistake.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[CORE]`
- **The naive approach forces the calling code to carry destination-specific context even though at that phase it just wants to upload; the code that actually knows the company is up at the start of the request.** — [J1f5b4vcxCQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1f5b4vcxCQ) `[STATED]`
